title: SLO-driven fidelity onboarding checklist and playbook
preview:
Overview

This document is a pragmatic playbook for onboarding customers onto SLO-driven fidelity tiers. text:
Overview

This document is a pragmatic playbook for onboarding customers onto SLO-driven fidelity tiers. It describes the mapping between customer SLOs (latency, availability, correctness), recommended fidelity tiers, required telemetry, operational checks, and a step-by-step checklist for commercial and technical leads. Use this when contracting customers for dedicated or private deployments and when migrating high-usage tenants from hosted to dedicated environments.

Audience

- Product managers and solutions engineering
- Customer success and account teams
- Platform and runtime engineers (serving, routing, telemetry)

Goals

1. Provide a repeatable alignment process that converts customer SLAs into recommended fidelity packages.
2. Ensure operational readiness (telemetry, runbooks, rollback) before committing to a fidelity onboarding date.
3. Minimize regressions in perceived qual...

text:
Overview

This page is a consolidated sales enablement handbook for GTM teams working with technical buyers evaluating Redwood Inference for conversational products. It couples user journey maps (developer and product-owner perspectives) with empirical price-sensitivity test templates and an evidence-backed competitive brief. The goal is to equip Sales Engineers, Solutions Architects, and AEs with repeatable conversation scripts, testable pricing scenarios, and clear security FAQ answers for prospect diligence.

Use cases in scope
- Real-time chatbots (sub-200ms token latency targets)
- Multi-turn assistants with long context (up to 64k tokens)
- High-throughput embedding and reranking pipelines
- Edge / hybrid deployments for regulated customers

Quick reference: what to carry to a technical call
1) Two-slide technical narrative (latency/cost tradeoffs + fallback story)
2) One-page p...

title: Rapid Authorization Containment and Evidence Orchestration Playbook

Overview:

Purpose: This playbook prescribes a fast, auditable, cross-functional process for containing authorization-related incidents (privilege escalation, token misuse, delegated-authorization failures), collecting compliance-grade evidence, and orchestrating multi-team handoffs while respecting data residency and retention constraints. It is intended for SREs, SecOps, on-call engineers, product security, and legal.

Scope: RBAC and auth incidents impacting production inference endpoints, control-plane configuration (roles, attribute stores, delegation), audit log integrity, or any event that may require regulatory evidence packaging (SOC2 / ISO / regional data residency). Excludes non-authorization incidents (use standard incident process).

Quick summary of goals:
- Contain active authorization exposure within 15 minutes for critical incidents.

Summary
-------
This dossier is a single-page commercial reference for technical partners (system integrators, ISVs, and channel partners) who co-sell or embed Redwood Inference. It bundles: concise positioning and messaging tailored to technical buyers, a short competitive brief, partner-facing pricing scenarios, a turn-key pilot and onboarding checklist, an outline for a partner-facing pitch deck, two anonymized case narratives, and a curated security FAQ that frequently appears during partner diligence. Use this during joint sales calls, partner enablement sessions, and pilot scoping.

Quick takeaways
- Core value proposition for partners: deliver predictable, production-ready LLM inference to customers while minimizing ops burden and preserving cost controls.
- Primary partner win conditions: predictable latency, controllable unit economics, auditability for regulated data, and si...

Overview

Purpose
This playbook defines the process, roles, SLAs, and artifacts required to onboard and support satellite teams, project rotations, and short-term contractors who require scoped access to Redwood systems. It pairs an access lifecycle with a mentor pairing program to accelerate impact while maintaining security and cost controls.

Scope
- Applies to any transient or temporary team event lasting between 2 days and 12 months: rotations, shadowing, pilot squads, contractor engagements, and hackweeks.
- Not intended for full-time hire onboarding (see new-hire orientation).
- Covers tool access (Okta, Redwood Console, GCP projects, Vault, Jira, GitHub org), mentor pairing, and deprovisioning.

Goals
- Reduce time-to-first-meaningful-commit for satellite contributors to under 2 business days for standard access and 4 hours for urgent business-critical cases.

title: Enterprise fidelity surcharge and grace period policy
preview:
Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference's enterprise fidelity surcharge policy and associated grace period rules. Its goal is to provide a predictable commercial mechanism for covering incremental cost when customers request fidelity guarantees (higher-cost model variants, pinned quantization levels, or rese...
text:
Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference's enterprise fidelity surcharge policy and associated grace period rules. Its goal is to provide a predictable commercial mechanism for covering incremental cost when customers request fidelity guarantees (higher-cost model variants, pinned quantization levels, or reserved warm capacity) while giving a clear transition window for customers migrating across fidelity bands.

Summary (one paragraph)
Enterprise customers may opt into fidelity guarantees for mission-critical routes. When a customer selects a fidelity guarantee that increases runtime cost materially (as defined in the Surcharge Thresholds table), a fidelity surcharge may be applied to their invoice. Customers receive a configurable grace period during onboarding or migration where surcharges are delayed, partly discounted, or phased in to reduce churn and provide time to optim...

title: SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure Playbook
preview:
Summary:

This playbook defines the SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure strategy for Redwood's serving runtime. It describes the design, configuration, operational runbook, and recommended testing/benchmarking steps to ensure tenant-level SLOs are respected under mixed workloads and overload scenarios. T...
text:
Summary:

This playbook defines the SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure strategy for Redwood's serving runtime. It describes the design, configuration, operational runbook, and recommended testing/benchmarking steps to ensure tenant-level SLOs are respected under mixed workloads and overload scenarios. The playbook targets runtime-admission (service), kernel-scheduler (service), and dispatcher components and is intended for on-call, runtime engineers, and SREs.

Goals and scope:

- Ensure per-tenant and per-route latency SLOs are prioritized when system capacity is constrained.
- Provide deterministic admission behavior that is auditable and testable.
- Define backpressure signals exposed to clients and internal services.
- Offer a practical operational runbook for detecting, mitigating, and rolling back admission policy changes.

Out of scope:

- Model-level quality regre...

title: Typed ID and Opaque Identifier Patterns for Inference APIs
preview:
Overview:
This document specifies recommended patterns for representing identifiers (IDs) in Redwood Inference services and SDKs. The goal is to increase type safety, reduce accidental cross-tenant or cross-resource ID mixing, make telemetry more explicit, and enable safer migrations between identifier formats. Thes...
text:
Overview:
This document specifies recommended patterns for representing identifiers (IDs) in Redwood Inference services and SDKs. The goal is to increase type safety, reduce accidental cross-tenant or cross-resource ID mixing, make telemetry more explicit, and enable safer migrations between identifier formats. These recommendations apply to public-facing inference APIs, internal service-to-service RPCs, and developer SDKs.

Why this matters:
- Simple untyped strings lead to accidental misuse (e.g., passing a model_id where a deployment_id is required).
- Observable signals and logs lose structure when different ID classes are conflated.
- Security and auditability improve when IDs are clearly typed and opaque where appropriate.
- Backwards-incompatible ID shape changes (e.g., adding versioning prefixes) are safer when wrapped by a typed adapter.

Scope:
- Applies to all new APIs and ...

Overview

This document describes recommended networking bridge and fallback patterns for Redwood Private (VPC and on‑prem) deployments. It focuses on field-safe connectivity designs that preserve control‑plane reachability, allow safe model hot‑swaps, and provide deterministic failure modes when network paths are constrained (air‑gapped customers, intermittent interconnects, or restricted egress environments).

Audience

- Deployment engineers executing customer installs
- Field operations and SRE teams responsible for day‑2 networking
- Solutions engineering and customer success for pre‑sales readiness checks

Scope and goals

- Provide a small set of vetted bridge and fallback patterns that balance security, observability, and reliability.
- Document rollout and rollback procedures for network modifications without requiring full service restarts.
- List concrete customer requireme...

preview:

text:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes an operational pattern and step-by-step procedure for re-allocating SLO budget and rapidly provisioning or shifting capacity to hot execution paths ("hot-path" services) during acute traffic events that threaten customer-facing SLOs. It combines manual escalation, deterministic automation gates, and verification checks so that SREs and paged responders can act quickly with low risk.

Scope and motivation
--------------------
- Applies to Redwood services that touch real-time inference and request-routing: inference-gateway, request-router, kv-cache, continuous-batcher, model-worker, and capacity-allocator.
- Use when an SLO is projecting to miss within the current burn window due to sudden load, model regression, or regional capacity loss.
- Goal: restore SLO headroom to a safe state within 10–30 minutes for high-severity incidents while preservi...

title: hallucination-stress-test-suite-and-recovery-playbook-2027
preview:

This document describes the Hallucination Stress Test Suite (HSTS) and an associated Recovery Playbook for operationalizing detection, triage, and automated mitigation of hallucination regressions across Redwood’s inference fleet (hosted, dedicated, private). The HSTS is intended to be integrated ...
text:
Overview
========

This document describes the Hallucination Stress Test Suite (HSTS) and an associated Recovery Playbook for operationalizing detection, triage, and automated mitigation of hallucination regressions across Redwood’s inference fleet (hosted, dedicated, private). The HSTS is intended to be integrated with the eval-mesh and probe-orchestrator pipelines to provide deterministic and stochastic stress probes that surface hallucination risk under real-world conditions: prompt priming changes, quantized kernels, KV-cache boundary events, and latency-preserving batching.

Goals
-----
- Provide a repeatable, versioned set of probes that exercise hallucination-prone behaviors.
- Define measurable metrics and pass/fail thresholds for gating rollouts.
- Prescribe triage steps and automated repair actions (prompt rewrites, quant-fallback, enhanced priming) to reduce incident MTTR.

title: Embedded Feature Simulator: SDK Onboarding Examples & Telemetry Codelets
preview:

The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, s...
text:
Overview

This page describes a lightweight, reproducible simulator pattern for embedding feature flows used in SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and support triage. The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, streaming, and local telemetry emission. It is intended to help SDK authors, support engineers, and sample-app integrators validate behavior against Redwood API expectations before full integration.

Why this exists

- Speed: launch a local stub that reproduces class of SDK runtime problems in under a minute.
- Determinism: control tokenization and response timing to exercise batching and timeout logic.
- Observability alignment: validate telemetry event formats and correlation IDs before customers escalate.
- Canonical fixes: include reproducible codelets that match support...

Overview:

This runbook describes Redwood's standardized procedure for applying predictive capacity throttling and performing a controlled canary rollback when early signals indicate SLO risk. It codifies the decision thresholds, required telemetry, automation hooks, and human escalation steps so teams can reduce blast radius while preserving customer-facing availability.

Purpose and scope:

Key definitions:
- Predictive throttle: automated traffic shaping that reduces request concurrency or drops non-critical request classes bas...

title: Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture
preview:
Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to...
text:
Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to central GPU pools.

Goals

- Provide a predictable low-latency path for small/short-text workloads (p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms) using local, quantized model shards.
- Retain correctness and quality by transparently fanouting requests to central inference for long-context or compute‑intensive queries.
- Ensure graceful degradation and clear rollback/upgrade paths for both local and central tiers.
- Minimize customer network egress and meet privacy constraints (models and tokens may remain within customer VPC).

High-level topology

1) Edge nodes (customer-managed in VPC or on-...

title: Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge Reference Architecture
preview:
Overview:
This reference architecture describes the Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge: a secure, operator-friendly pattern for capturing tenant traffic traces, KV-cache snapshots, and control-plane metadata from private VPC and on-prem Redwood Private deployments for post-incident analysis, regulatory audit...
text:
Overview:
This reference architecture describes the Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge: a secure, operator-friendly pattern for capturing tenant traffic traces, KV-cache snapshots, and control-plane metadata from private VPC and on-prem Redwood Private deployments for post-incident analysis, regulatory audits, and model-drift investigations. The design balances minimal performance impact, strong chain-of-custody guarantees, and customer privacy controls.

Goals:
- Provide an immutable, auditable snapshot pipeline for tenant state and request traces.
- Ensure snapshots can be collected with <=5% added tail-latency under normal load via asynchronous capture.
- Maintain tenant data isolation: all forensic snapshots are encrypted with customer KMS/HSM keys where required.
- Offer deterministic retention and evidence packaging for audits (customer-facing artifact).

When to use thi...

title: Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator
preview:

ACIR-SIG produces standardized, auditable customer incident summaries within a defined SLA window after an incid...
text:
Summary:

This document defines the design, operational runbook, verification checks, and customer-facing templates for Redwood's Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator (ACIR-SIG). ACIR-SIG produces standardized, auditable customer incident summaries within a defined SLA window after an incident is classified as customer-impacting. The system computes SLO impact automatically from telemetry and timeline evidence, assembles a concise narrative, and supports automated delivery to customers (via Console, email, or support ticket) with human-in-the-loop gating.

Goals and scope:
- Goal: Reduce time-to-customer-summary while preserving accuracy, traceability, and blameless language.
- Scope: infra and inference incidents that affect production API responses (latency, availability, errors). Excludes: billing, account-level policy, or non-production incidents.
- Outpu...

Overview

The lab is designed for platform operators, SREs, and reliability engineers who run postmortems, runbooks, and training.

title: Fallback validation and chaos test plan for graceful runtime fallbacks
preview:
Purpose
-------
This document defines a repeatable validation and chaos-testing plan for the runtime's graceful fallback paths. It is intended to: verify correctness and SLA-preservation when primary kernels, model variants, or regions become unavailable; exercise fallthrough policies and automated rollback; and val...
text:
Purpose
-------
This document defines a repeatable validation and chaos-testing plan for the runtime's graceful fallback paths. It is intended to: verify correctness and SLA-preservation when primary kernels, model variants, or regions become unavailable; exercise fallthrough policies and automated rollback; and validate telemetry/alert fidelity used for automated mitigation. The goal is to catch correctness, performance regressions, and observability gaps before customer impact.

Scope
-----
- Components: kernel selector, request router, model-variant manager, KV cache coordination, shard handoff logic, admission controller, and routing fallbacks (region/model/tier).
- Test goals: functional correctness (response validity), latency SLO preservation (p50/p95/p99 targets), cost/capacity boundary behavior, and signal hygiene (trace/span/metric availability).
- Exclusions: hardware-level...

title: PRD: Latency Budgeting, Customer Contracts, and First‑Party SLOs
preview:

The feature — Latency Budgeting & Contracting — lets customers: (a) declare per‑route latency ...
text:
Summary
-------
This PRD defines the product scope, customer contracts, and operational controls required to introduce first‑party Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for latency across Redwood’s Hosted and Dedicated surfaces. The feature — Latency Budgeting & Contracting — lets customers: (a) declare per‑route latency targets, (b) bind those targets to billing/credit commitments, and (c) opt into automated route shaping and fallback policies that protect higher‑value traffic.

Goals and non‑goals
------------------
Primary goals:
- Provide a clear, enforceable lifecycle for per‑route latency SLOs (declare → observe → enforce → invoice).
- Enable commercial contracts that map latency tiers to pricing credits and capacity reservations.
- Offer runtime controls (shaping, prefetched capacity, graceful degradation) so customers achieve SLOs without manual operations.

Non‑goals for this PRD:
...

title: ADR: request watermarking and SLO-preserving shedding
preview:
Summary
-------
This ADR establishes 'request watermarking' as the runtime protocol and a coordinated shedding policy to preserve tail latency SLOs under high-load or degraded GPU capacity. Watermarking attaches lightweight budget and fidelity metadata to each inference request at the API edge, enabling downstream r...
text:
Summary
-------
This ADR establishes 'request watermarking' as the runtime protocol and a coordinated shedding policy to preserve tail latency SLOs under high-load or degraded GPU capacity. Watermarking attaches lightweight budget and fidelity metadata to each inference request at the API edge, enabling downstream runtime actors (scheduler, KV cache, and model router) to make deterministic, SLO-aware shedding and fidelity decisions.

Motivation
----------
Redwood serves heterogeneous workloads with strict 99th/99.9th percentile latency SLOs on shared GPU pools. When capacity is constrained (bursting, partial GPU failures, or noisy neighbors) indiscriminate admission control or FIFO shedding causes disproportionate user impact and cascading quality regressions. We need a deterministic, observable, and composable mechanism that: 
- Encodes client intent and budget on each request so run...

title: Mitigation: Provisional credit reversal causing autopay hold (RevRec & Billing)
preview:
## Purpose
This page documents the operational mitigation and finance/legal guidance for incidents where a support-issued provisional credit is automatically reversed during reconciliation, leading to a negative invoice balance and an autopay hold. It consolidates learnings from SUP-121212 (Acme Health) and establis...
text:
## Purpose
This page documents the operational mitigation and finance/legal guidance for incidents where a support-issued provisional credit is automatically reversed during reconciliation, leading to a negative invoice balance and an autopay hold. It consolidates learnings from SUP-121212 (Acme Health) and establishes a repeatable response for RevRec, Billing Engineering, and Customer Support.

## Background (what happened)
In SUP-121212, a provisional credit issued for hourly smoothing was reversed automatically during an overnight/hourly reconciliation run. The reversal produced a negative balance on the customer invoice and triggered an automated autopay hold. For a dedicated customer, the payment-hold state also restricted production API keys and degraded integrations.

### Reference timeline (example case: Acme Health)
- 2026-03-10 22:14 UTC: Support issued provisional credit (R...

title: Few-shot priming economics and sample compression guide
preview:

It balances quality (coherence, instruction-following) against unit-costs (tokens per probe, annotation overhead) and operational constraints (throughput, latency for streaming ...
text:
Summary:
This guide documents Redwood's recommended approach to designing few-shot prompts and compressed sample sets for production evals. It balances quality (coherence, instruction-following) against unit-costs (tokens per probe, annotation overhead) and operational constraints (throughput, latency for streaming evals). The guidance is intended for evaluator engineers, model-onboarding owners, and probe stewards.

Goals:
- Reduce per-probe token cost by 20–60% without regressing key quality slices (intent accuracy, safety sentinel pass-rate).
- Provide an auditable compression workflow so that downstream continuous evals and shadow deployments use reproducible priming sets.
- Define metrics and an acceptance gate for compressed few-shot sets.

Background and tradeoffs:
Few-shot priming increases model context and improves alignment accuracy on many instruction tasks, but it also in...

Overview:

This runbook defines the Tenant Impact Scorer (TIS), a deterministic scoring algorithm and automation flow that:
- Quantifies incident impact at the tenant/customer level.
- Converts impact into a prioritized remediation level (P0..P3).
- Drives automated gating of non-critical deployments and throttles via the gatekeeper-service when error budgets or capacity signals cross thresholds.

Goal:
- Reduce time-to-prioritize for multi-tenant incidents.
- Ensure capacity/economics aware mitigation (avoid global throttles for a small set of high-error tenants).
- Provide a reproducible signal for oncall and product owners to take action.

Scope:
- Applies to all customer-facing inference endpoints and long-running background jobs that surface tenant errors (service prefixes: inference-api/*, embeddings/*, reranking/*).

title: Tiered-fallback-routing-and-eviction-architecture-2026
preview:
Overview:

This document describes the Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) used by Redwood’s serving runtime to meet multi-dimensional SLOs (latency percentiles, cost budget, and availability) while degrading predictably during capacity pressure or downstream failure modes. TFREA unifies routin...
text:
Overview:

This document describes the Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) used by Redwood’s serving runtime to meet multi-dimensional SLOs (latency percentiles, cost budget, and availability) while degrading predictably during capacity pressure or downstream failure modes. TFREA unifies routing policies, candidate model variants, eviction heuristics, and runtime-informed kernel scheduling hints into a single, observable control loop.

Goals:
- Maintain 99th-percentile latency SLOs per route under normal load.
- Bound hosted per-token cost within configured customer cost tiers.
- Provide deterministic, monotonic quality degradation (higher-quality -> lower-quality) with automated rollback when pressure subsides.
- Make fallback decisions explainable and traceable per request for post-incident analysis.

Scope:
- Applies to hosted and Dedicated modes; Private depl...

title: Cross-region signal fusion and fallback runbook
preview:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. text:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The goal is to preserve customer-facing SLOs (p95 latency < 300ms for interactive routes; token cost drift alerts at > +30% delta) while limiting unnecessary cross-region failovers and cost overruns.

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.

Signal sources and priorities
----------------------------
1) Latency...

title: Operator Interactive Regression Debugger — Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes the Operator Interactive Regression Debugger: a low-latency, human-in-the-loop protocol and toolkit for investigating production-quality regressions that show up in eval observability or customer signals. It complements automated bisect and probe tooling by enabling an operator or en...
text:
Summary:
This playbook describes the Operator Interactive Regression Debugger: a low-latency, human-in-the-loop protocol and toolkit for investigating production-quality regressions that show up in eval observability or customer signals. It complements automated bisect and probe tooling by enabling an operator or engineer to rapidly confirm causal factors, inspect intermediate artifacts (prompts, tokenization, KV cache traces), and run guarded micro-rollouts or targeted mitigations.

Scope:
- Intended for use by SREs, Applied ML engineers, and on-call evaluators responding to model quality regressions or severe fidelity shifts.
- Covers interactive investigation steps, CLI examples, automated helper scripts, telemetry checks, and safe rollback/mitigation actions.
- Not a replacement for automated bisect; use after initial triage if regression persists or is high-severity.

When to use...

text:
Summary:
This guide defines a repeatable design and validation workflow for authoring prompts when you have hard latency or token-budget constraints. It covers principled prompt composition, token accounting, cache-priming patterns, integration tests for the eval harness, and rollout checks for model promotion. The target reader is engineers and applied-ML owners preparing prompts for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).

Scope and objectives:
- Scope: customer-facing text- and chat-style prompts used in production inference paths (single-turn and multi-turn). Does not cover multimodal binary payload formats.
- Objectives:
 - Ensure prompts meet latency SLOs and token budgets for cost predictability.
 - Maximize reuse of KV/prefix caches and enable safe quantized deployments.
 - Provide testable templates and an automated validation checklist for onboarding.

title: Entitlement Chronicle and Log Preservation Guidance
preview:
## Overview

Purpose: This document defines the "Entitlement Chronicle" concept — a canonical, time-ordered record of entitlement lifecycle events (grants, revocations, role changes, delegation), and the operational guidance for preserving authorization-related telemetry and artifacts to satisfy compliance, incident...
text:
## Overview

Purpose: This document defines the "Entitlement Chronicle" concept — a canonical, time-ordered record of entitlement lifecycle events (grants, revocations, role changes, delegation), and the operational guidance for preserving authorization-related telemetry and artifacts to satisfy compliance, incident response, and forensic needs. It covers log capture, retention/archival policy, chain-of-custody procedures, SDLC integration points, and runbook steps for producing compliance evidence bundles.

Scope: Applies to all Redwood Inference services and control planes that issue or enforce entitlements (console authn/authz, service-to-service IAM, admin UI, provisioning pipelines, and third-party plugins). Does not replace existing retention policies for privacy-sensitive user content — it augments audit retention with explicit custody and preservation steps for authz telemetry...

title: SLO to Customer Impact Classifier and Remediation Workflow
preview:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident rev...
text:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 200ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defi...

title: Service poison-pill safety and isolation guide
preview:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service ...
text:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service degradation (examples: pathological prompts that exhaust KV caches, malformed inputs that trigger infinite loops in model runtime, or tenant-driven token-surges).

Scope & applicability
---------------------
Applies to: inference-gateway, batching-worker, kv-prefix-cache, model-eval-sandbox, and the routing layer (router and model-variant-fallbacker). Does NOT replace tenant-level security policies; complements quota and billing controls.

Goals
-----
- Detect poison-pill behavior quickly with deterministic signals.
- Isolate affected tenants/requests before systemic impact...

title: Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI) and Annotator Adaptation Manual
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI), describes a standardized annotator adaptation protocol, and provides operational runbooks for integrating QSPI into our promotion/evaluation pipeline for quantized artifacts. The goal is to detect, quantify, and remediate semantic or t...
text:
Summary:
This document defines the Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI), describes a standardized annotator adaptation protocol, and provides operational runbooks for integrating QSPI into our promotion/evaluation pipeline for quantized artifacts. The goal is to detect, quantify, and remediate semantic or task-specific signal loss introduced by quantization changes while minimizing annotation overhead and preserving production fidelity.

Why this matters:
- Quantization reduces cost but can change subtle tokenization/representation behavior that impacts downstream labelers, rerankers, and function-call correctness.
- Existing throughput-driven checks (latency/peak tokens) miss graded signal degradation affecting annotator agreement and quality SLAs.
- QSPI gives a single, explainable index and a fast path to adapt annotators and probe sets where signal loss is concentrated.

...

The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements whi...
text:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements while preserving quality and observability contracts. This document ties together: eval-harness checks, prompt and benchmark selection, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, quantization profile validation, and regression triage steps.

Scope

- Applies to any model promotion that changes runtime numeric representation (FP16/FP32 -> INT8/PACT/4-bit) for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).
- Includes single-model migrations and model-variant rollouts where a quantized variant will be routed by policy.
- Excludes research-only artifacts and offline-only e...

- Platform/Cloud Architect: evaluates network, VPC, and deployment models (hosted vs dedicated vs private).
- Procurement / Legal: looks for pricing predictability, SLAs, and security/compliance evidence.

Key positioning and messaging (Retail-focused)

This playbook consolidates recommended SDK patterns for streaming + batching usage with Redwood Inference APIs, a short set of quickstart templates for client teams, and canonical fixes for the top support issues we observe in the field. Intended audience:

- SDK maintainers and support engineers.

When to pick a kit vs full quickstart

title: ADR - Telemetry Stencil & Feature-Gate Emission Contract
preview:
Summary:
This ADR defines a compact, enforceable contract between runtime feature gates (feature flags/rollouts) and telemetry producers. We introduce the concept of a 'telemetry stencil' - a minimal, versioned emission schema that accompanies each gated feature. The stencil ensures predictable ingestion cost, bound...
text:
Summary:
This ADR defines a compact, enforceable contract between runtime feature gates (feature flags/rollouts) and telemetry producers. We introduce the concept of a 'telemetry stencil' - a minimal, versioned emission schema that accompanies each gated feature. The stencil ensures predictable ingestion cost, bounded cardinality, and privacy-safe defaults while enabling meaningful observability during staged rollouts.

Problem statement and motivation:
- Incidents in 2024-2025 exposed two repeating failure modes: runaway telemetry cardinality from experimental features and accidental emission of PII fields when new gate logic touched request structures. Both increased ingestion costs and created compliance risk.
- Teams want low-friction rollouts but need guardrails that prevent telemetry spikes, make ownership explicit, and integrate with rollout automation (canary, staged percentag...

title: Multi-tenant Shared NAT & Egress Crediting Allocator — Design, IaC, and Runbook
preview:

-------

The Allocator provides fair, auditable egress crediting and NAT port assignment for tenant workloads that share a pool of NAT g...
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes the architecture, Terraform/IaC patterns, monitoring, and on-call runbook for a Multi-tenant Shared NAT + Egress Crediting Allocator (the Allocator). The Allocator provides fair, auditable egress crediting and NAT port assignment for tenant workloads that share a pool of NAT gateways (or egress appliances). It is intended for Redwood-hosted GPU and inference tenant workloads that must control egress spend while preserving tenant isolation and predictable QoS.

Goals
-----
- Meter per-tenant egress at high cardinality (per account / environment / cluster) with sub-minute accuracy for billing and throttling.
- Enforce predictable fairness by allocating reusable NAT port ranges and egress credit quotas per tenant.
- Provide a Terraform module and CI/CD pattern for managing allocator pools across accounts/regions.
- Offer an on-call runbook that red...

title: Private upgrades: rollback loop (RRB-17) Detection, workaround, and fix
preview:
# Overview
This page documents the "rollback loop" failure mode in Redwood Private upgrades tracked as **RRB-17** (also described as "stuck rollback" or "rollback keeps re-triggering").

This is customer-impacting because the installer can retry rollbacks indefinitely, leaving the cluster in a non-converged state.

...
text:
# Overview
This page documents the "rollback loop" failure mode in Redwood Private upgrades tracked as **RRB-17** (also described as "stuck rollback" or "rollback keeps re-triggering").

This is customer-impacting because the installer can retry rollbacks indefinitely, leaving the cluster in a non-converged state.

# Symptoms
- Console/installer status shows **Rollback in progress** for >30 minutes
- Installer controller logs repeat messages like "scheduling rollback retry"
- No forward progress after multiple rollback attempts

# Affected versions
- Observed in: 2.9.0 upgrades from 2.8.0
- Potentially affects other upgrade paths when CRD migration partially applies

# Detection
## Log indicators
Search installer-controller logs for repeated sequences:
- `status=FAILED`
- `scheduling rollback retry`
- references to `installer-rollback-lock`

## Operational threshold
Treat as rollback ...

preview:

It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency...
text:
Summary

This document defines the safety contract, telemetry requirements, and a step-by-step split-testing procedure for rolling out new kernel variants in the serving runtime. It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency, correctness, and resource-stability properties while providing deterministic rollback triggers.

Goals

- Allow safe A/B (split) testing of kernel variants (e.g., tiled-attention-v2, quantized-matmul-approx, async-dispatch) against production workloads.
- Define automated and human-reviewed rollback criteria that preserve customer SLOs and prevent resource exhaustion.
- Standardize telemetry and alerting required to make split decisions reproducible and auditable.
- Provide a compact, executable checklist for runbook-driven canary experiments.

Scope and non-goals

- Scop...

title: Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan
preview:
Summary
This page defines the Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan: a standardized, automated validation and exercise suite for verifying that service contracts (API request/response schemas, streaming semantics, model-variant compatibility) survive realistic failure and degradation modes, and that grace...
text:
Summary
This page defines the Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan: a standardized, automated validation and exercise suite for verifying that service contracts (API request/response schemas, streaming semantics, model-variant compatibility) survive realistic failure and degradation modes, and that graceful-degradation policies keep downstream applications safe.

Purpose and scope
- Purpose: Ensure changes to APIs, model variants, runtime kernels, or infra do not cause silent client breakage or unacceptable user-facing quality regressions when the platform degrades (capacity loss, quantization fallbacks, network partitions, high tail latency).
- Scope: Applies to: Redwood public REST/streaming API endpoints, model-serving runtime variants (fp16, int8, quantized kernels), routing/fallback policies, and platform-side contract transforms (stream chunking, partial result check...

Overview

This playbook defines the field acceptance and safety certification criteria for offline / air‑gapped private deployments of the Redwood runtime. It is intended for delivery engineers, customer site operators, and SREs who perform the final acceptance verification and produce evidence for compliance packages.

Scope

- Applies to: on‑prem or isolated VPC deployments where control‑plane connectivity is intentionally restricted (air‑gapped or dark site).
- Excludes: hosted Redwood API, fully connected Dedicated deployments.
- Goal: provide a deterministic, repeatable test suite and sign‑off template to ensure the deployment meets safety, availability, and traceability requirements prior to customer handover.

Quick summary of acceptance outcomes (must all PASS to sign off)

1) Functional health: all core services report healthy and APIs respond to local queries.

text:
Summary:
This document defines the tenant-level contract between Redwood and enterprise customers for automated failover, observability telemetry, and cost-safeguard behavior. It is intended for customer success, platform engineers, SREs, and partner integration teams.

Goals:
- Keep customer-facing inference traffic available during service/resource degradation with predictable, auditable behavior.
- Ensure minimal false-positive escalation by defining telemetry thresholds and synthetic checks.
- Provide clear economic safeguards to prevent runaway billing while preserving critical availability.

Scope:
- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants only (hosted tier has separate defaults).
- Covers ingress failover, model fallback, burst throttling, billing-protection limits, and telemetry signals used for QBRs.
- Excludes cross-region DR for air-gapped on-prem installs unless explicitl...

Overview

This playbook describes a structured, automation-first approach for: (1) ensuring RBAC and authorization changes are safe to deploy, (2) capturing immutable compliance evidence across failures and cross-border failovers, and (3) integrating audit logging, data-residency constraints, and SDLC gating into incident response and risk exception processes. - Exclusions: ad-hoc developer test accounts (see Access Sandbox policy).

text:
Overview

This document defines Redwood Inference’s cross-functional governance lattice: a compact, operational catalog of company-wide policies and the workflow templates teams must use for access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement and expenses, vendor management, and travel. It is intended for process owners, managers, and auditors who run or depend on these corporate functions.

Scope

- Applies to all full-time employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to Redwood systems and data.
- Covers the following policy families: Access & Permissions, Change Management, Data Management, Procurement & Expenses, Vendor Management, Travel, and shared Templates used by teams.

Why a governance lattice?

title: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiati...
text:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA wi...
